items.correct: A unit of DISTANCE - the distance light travels in one year.
items.why: Students hear 'year' and assume time. Light year is distance (about 9.46 trillion km) - it's a useful astronomical scale, not a measure of when something happened.

items.q: What does a telescope's aperture primarily determine?
items.why: Students think bigger telescopes magnify more. Magnification depends on eyepiece. Aperture determines how much light you collect (faint objects visible) and resolution (fine detail visible). Big aperture = big telescope.

items.q: What determines whether a star becomes a white dwarf or a neutron star?
items.correct: Its mass - low/medium mass becomes a white dwarf; massive stars produce neutron stars or black holes.
items.misconception: Its colour during its main sequence phase.
items.why: Students think colour determines end state. Mass is the key factor - low-mass stars (like the Sun) end as white dwarfs; high-mass stars supernova, leaving neutron stars or black holes depending on mass.

items.q: How many planets are in the Solar System (IAU 2006)?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 8. “12” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who proposed the heliocentric model in 1543?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Copernicus. “Galileo” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximately how many million kilometres is 1 AU?
items.why: Exact answer: 150 million km. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
